text: I don't disagree that it's easier to get up to a "good" speed, but in terms of going fast, the movement of running appears (to me) to be much more efficient than skating. each stride in running propels the runner forward. feet, legs and arms move in a linear manner in the direction the runner is going. while it's physically taxing, and top speed is short lived, there is little wasted movement. each stride in skating one loses energy and movement side to side (strides are not just propelling forward) and the the friction (however little) to the skate gliding over the ice. skates & legs push out and back, arms slightly side to side. while it's certainly easier for me to skate at a "fast" pace, longer than I could ever run, I would assume that has a lot to do with the fact that as soon as I stop running I STOP, where as I continue forward/coasting if I stop skating. it would be awsome to have a race, or have someone work the actual physics/kenitics of the two, to see which is capable of a faster top speed, quickest, etc.
label: analysis

text: In total yards allowed and points per game the Rams are very close (19th and 18th ranked). The Saints are significantly better defensively (13th and 10th). I don't know how to explain it, but it's a fact that teams with bad defenses don't win super bowls very often. It could be because most of a teams wins usually come against teams with losing records. For example, of the Chiefs 11 wins, 8 are against teams with losing records. The Rams and Saints are similar. Which means any given teams performance against other good teams (which all their playoff opponents will be) is much different than their regular season record. Also, of the 3-4 games you have to win sequentially, 1 or 2 of them is going to be against a team with 2 weeks of time to prepare for you a full season of film to watch; which makes your weaknesses bigger and your strengths smaller. Also, good defenses are more likely to generate turnovers and turnover ratio is one of the more reliable metrics for determining who will win any given game.

text: As a former light infantryman who served in the 90’s and was mostly used for humanitarian missions I can jump in. The answer is: it’s complicated. As a young dude trained to kill, I absolutely hated that we were used to clean up trash in places like Haiti. I wanted to fight. But as an old dude now, seeing the issues that those that saw lots of combat have, I feel mostly lucky. If I had gone to basic/ait when I graduated instead of much later in the year, there is a pretty good chance I would have gone to the clusterfuck that was Somalia. Instead I rolled up to my duty station (10th Mtn Div) as a replacement for people who had actually been killed. That was a big deal in the mid 90’s. We were a decade away from the second gulf war and Afghanistan. Conflicts my unit deployed heavily to. So yeah there is a bit of an imposter feeling, but if I’m brutally honest, I feel lucky. I have enough back problems already, without possibly adding more.

text: For active duty military, mortality is 94.9 per 100,000 individuals per year. That’s 0.0949%.
